coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots with tunable excitation-independent fluorescence and organelle-specific targeting via core graphitization and surface groups engineering
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Ming-Yuan
Tian, Bao-Hua
Li, Xin-Xin
Li, Yu
Lei, Yu
Guo, Xi-Lin
Miao, Qiang
Li, Hao
Liang, Hai-Xia
coredata.dc:description: Single-emission fluorescence and non-specific cellular organelle-targeting limit the application of carbon dots (CDs) in bio-imaging. Here, we proposed an adjustable strategy for synthesis of four CDs: Tm1-, Tm2-, Tma-, and Tp-CDs. The synthetic scheme was designed with tartaric acid and m-/p-phenylenediamine as precursors and water/acetone as solvent through hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis and (or) column chromatography isolation. The maximum productivity of the CDs was 56.2 % and the highest fluorescence quantum yield was 47.8 %. These CDs emitted excitation-independent blue, green, and yellow fluorescence, respectively. The red shift of CDs fluorescence was caused by increasing graphitized conjugated sp2 domain and graphitic nitrogen content of carbon core. The hydrophilicity/lipophilicity and protonation ability of CDs determined their cellular uptake, intracellular trafficking and subcellular targets, including endoplasmic reticulum, lysosomes and nucleus. These CDs have the potential application in fluorescence visualization of live bacteria and in the organelle-targeted imaging of living mammalian, fungal, and plant cells. The study provides novel insights into rational design of CDs with multi-color fluorescence and organelle-specific targeting in live cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon Dots
Synthesis Strategy
Tunable Fluorescence
Organelle Targeting
Bioimaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of Tm1-, Tm2-, Tma-, and Tp-CDs. (a) The synthesis strategy of four CDs: hydrothermal synthesis of Tm-CDs from TA and mPD; isolation of Tm-CDs to obtain Tm1- and Tm2-CDs; change of solvent to acetone to obtain Tma-CDs; change of precursor to pPD to obtain Tp-CDs. (b-e) TEM images with the inset of particle size distribution and HR-TEM image. (f) XRD patterns. (g) Raman spectra. (h) FT-IR spectra.
High-resolution (a) C 1s, (b) N 1s, and (c) O 1s spectra, (d) 13C NMR and (e) 1H NMR spectra, and (f) schematic diagrams of carbon core and surface groups of Tm1-, Tm2-, Tma-, and Tp-CDs.

Fluorescence spectral characteristics of Tm1-, Tm2-, Tma-, and Tp-CDs. (a-d) 3D FL spectra. The insets show maximal excitation/emission spectra and photographs of CDs under daylight and UV light at 365 nm irradiation. (e) UV–Vis absorption spectra. (f) Mechanism diagrams of redshift in fluorescence.

Fluorescence imaging with Tm-, Tm1-, Tm2-, Tma-, and Tp-CDs and colocalization analysis. (a) Fluorescence images under multi-channel excitation. (b) Fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells staining with CDs and commercialized fluorescence dye, and dual-fluorescence intensity analysis of region of interest. Pearson correlation coefficients (PCCs) analysis of colocalization of (c) CDs with commercial organelle dyes in various cells, and (d) CDs with other organelle dyes.

Effects of endocytosis inhibition on cellular uptake of Tm1-, Tm2-, Tma-, and Tp-CDs. (a) Fluorescence images and (b) mean fluorescence intensity of regions of interest (n = 100), of MCF-7 cells staining with CDs with/without endocytosis inhibitors treatment.

Time-lapse imaging of MCF-7 cells staining with CDs and specific organelle dyes. Subcellular distribution and schematic diagrams of (a) Tm1-, (b) Tm2-, (c) Tma-, and (d) Tp-CDs at different time-points after staining.

Dynamic monitoring of organelles with CDs. Fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells stained (a) with Tm1-CDs and PI at pH 4.5, (b) with Tm2-CDs at different stage of mitosis, and (c) with Tma-CDs at different time-points (green fluorescence replaced by purple fluorescence to distinguish lysosome locations in adjacent images).

Fluorescence imaging of bacterial, fungal and plant live cells stained with CDs. Fluorescence images of (a) six bacteria, (c) two fungi and (d) onion epidermal cells, stained with Tm1-, Tm2-, Tma-, and Tp-CDs. (b) Fluorescence images of P. aeruginosa co-stained with Tm1-, Tm2-, and Tp-CDs.
The cellular uptake, intracellular trafficking, and localization of these CDs.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots with tunable excitation-independent fluorescence and organelle-specific targeting via core graphitization and surface groups engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Biomedical Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, 209 University Street, Jinzhong, Shanxi 030600, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Biomedical Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, 209 University Street, Jinzhong, Shanxi 030600, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Ecology, Taiyuan University of Technology, 79 West Street Yingze, Taiyuan, Shanxi 030024, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Ecology, Taiyuan University of Technology, 79 West Street Yingze, Taiyuan, Shanxi 030024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Electronic Information and Optical Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, 209 University Street, Jinzhong, Shanxi 030600, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Electronic Information and Optical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Electronic Information and Optical Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, 209 University Street, Jinzhong, Shanxi 030600, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Biomedical Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, 209 University Street, Jinzhong, Shanxi 030600, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Four types of CDs were efficiently synthesized through an integrated synthesis strategy, achieving dual control of blue-to-yellow fluorescence emission and precise organelle targeting, and demonstrating their versatility in bioimaging applications.

Single-emission fluorescence and non-specific cellular organelle-targeting limit the application of carbon dots (CDs) in bio-imaging. Here, we proposed an adjustable strategy for synthesis of four CDs: Tm1-, Tm2-, Tma-, and Tp-CDs. The synthetic scheme was designed with tartaric acid and m-/p-phenylenediamine as precursors and water/acetone as solvent through hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis and (or) column chromatography isolation. The maximum productivity of the CDs was 56.2 % and the highest fluorescence quantum yield was 47.8 %. These CDs emitted excitation-independent blue, green, and yellow fluorescence, respectively. The red shift of CDs fluorescence was caused by increasing graphitized conjugated sp2 domain and graphitic nitrogen content of carbon core. The hydrophilicity/lipophilicity and protonation ability of CDs determined their cellular uptake, intracellular trafficking and subcellular targets, including endoplasmic reticulum, lysosomes and nucleus. These CDs have the potential application in fluorescence visualization of live bacteria and in the organelle-targeted imaging of living mammalian, fungal, and plant cells. The study provides novel insights into rational design of CDs with multi-color fluorescence and organelle-specific targeting in live cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Research has shown that organelle dysfunction can lead to various diseases, including the association of endoplasmic reticulum (ER) impairment with neurodegenerative diseases, lysosomal malfunction with lysosomal storage diseases, and nuclear abnormalities with hereditary diseases [1–3]. Real-time monitoring of organelle changes can provide strong support for the early diagnosis and treatment of related diseases. Therefore, visualizing organelles is of significant importance. Currently, fluorescent nanomaterials such as organic dyes, semiconductor quantum dots, and noble metal-based nanoclusters are used in various fields. However, their disadvantages are increasingly apparent, with their complex organic or heavy metal compositions exhibiting biotoxicity and high production costs due to complex synthesis processes. Additionally, they often lack organelle-targeting capabilities, limiting their use in biomedicine [4–6]. Therefore, the development of new fluorescent nanotargeting materials is urgent.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a class of fluorescent nanomaterials with simple synthesis routes, good biocompatibility, excellent cell permeability, and high photostability, are among the most attractive candidates in the field of biological fluorescence imaging [7,8]. The fluorescence of CDs is related to the carbon core state controlled by conjugated sp2 domains, the surface and edge states controlled by chemical groups attached to the carbon skeleton, the molecular state controlled by organic fluorescent groups, and the crosslinking-enhanced emission effect controlled by sub-fluorescent groups [9–12]. These can usually be adjusted by changing the reaction conditions. Meanwhile, the inherent properties of CDs or surface modifications can achieve organelle targeting. It has been reported that CDs with amphiphilic/ionized surfaces or nuclear localization signal (NLS) peptides have nuclear targeting capabilities [13,14]. Amine-rich CDs modified with triphenylphosphine (TPP) target mitochondria, while CDs with a thiol-rich surface target the Golgi apparatus [15,16]. CDs modified with precursors rich in basic groups or morpholine (ML) derivatives achieve lysosomal localization [17,18]. CDs with electrophilic/lipophilic properties or laurylamine modifications target the ER [19,20]. Therefore, rationally designing synthesis schemes based on the known CDs structures' effects on fluorescence emission/subcellular localization, and then exploring their complex structure–activity relationships based on the experimental results, is an efficient research strategy.

This study focuses on designing efficient synthesis routes to control the graphitized carbon core structure and surface functional groups of CDs to achieve dual regulation of excitation-independent fluorescence and organelle-specific targeting. In generally, the structure of CDs is composed of a carbon core and oxygen/nitrogen-containing surface groups (O- and N- groups) [21]. It has been shown that adjusting the graphitized conjugated sp2 domain containing heteroatoms in the carbon core is an effective means to achieve different fluorescence emissions, while hydrophilicity/lipophilicity and protonation influenced by surface functional groups are considered key to targeting organelles [22,23]. Typically, the properties of CDs obtained by the bottom-up approach of dehydrating and polymerizing small organic molecules are highly related to the raw materials, playing an important role in the formation of luminescent centers and the preservation of surface functional groups. Using tartaric acid (TA) and phenylenediamine (PD) as precursors, the former provides a negative charge and hydrophilicity, while the latter provides a positive charge as well as a conjugatable phenyl group and N doping. It is predicted that synthesizing CDs with different carbon core structures and surface properties through different pathways can achieve multicolor fluorescence and multi-organelle targeting. Based on this principle, we successfully achieved the fluorescence transition of CDs from blue to yellow and specific targeting of the ER, lysosomes, and nucleus. Using CDs as fluorescent probes, we realized the monitoring of organelles and multicolor fluorescence visualization of live bacteria. Concurrently, structural fluorescence imaging of fungi and plant cells was achieved. Therefore, this provides a useful tool for scientific research on different biological species.
For details on materials and methods, see the Supporting Information file.

Four CDs were obtained by adjustment of the hydrothermal synthesis of Tm-CDs from TA and m-phenylenediamine (mPD) (Fig. 1 a). Tm1- and Tm2-CDs were isolated from Tm-CDs by column chromatography, which were formed due to heterogeneous polymerization in the aqueous phase. To enhance the dehydration effect, acetone was used as the solvent for solvothermal synthesis of Tma-CDs. To change N-doping, p-phenylenediamine (pPD) was used to replace mPD for hydrothermal synthesis of Tp-CDs. The productivity of Tm1-, Tm2-, Tma-, and Tp-CDs were 17.8 %, 21.7 %, 56.2 %, and 40.3 %, respectively.

TEM images showed that all these CDs were spherical and uniform, with average diameters of 2.9, 4.6, 5.0, and 7.2 nm, respectively (Fig. 1b–e). The clear lattice fringe with an in-plane lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (inset of Fig. 1b–e) and the broad peak around 2θ = 24.8° (Fig. 1f) corresponded to (100) and (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon, respectively, indicating the presence of a high-crystalline graphitic carbon in each CDs [24,25]. The Raman spectra (Fig. 1g) showed characteristic D and G band at about 1352 and 1595 cm−1, respectively, which were assigned to disordered sp3 carbon and graphitic sp2 carbon. The D and G band intensity ratio (IG/ID) values for Tm1-, Tm2-, Tma-, and Tp-CDs were progressively increasing, suggesting an enhancement in their graphitization degree and an increase in the size of conjugated sp2 domains [26,27].

Surface groups of these CDs were characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 1h). Tm1-CDs exhibited obvious absorption bands of C-H (2800–3000 cm−1), whereas Tm2-CDs did not show these bands [28]. The higher content of lipid-soluble aliphatic hydrocarbon in Tm1-CDs compared to Tm2-CDs facilitated their selective extraction using ethyl acetate–methanol mixtures with volume ratios of 20:1 and 1:1, respectively. Tma-CDs displayed the most pronounced characteristic peaks of the CH bond, confirming enhanced dehydration polymerization by acetone. The significantly enhanced amide CN (1335 cm−1) and amide CO (1673 cm−1) stretching vibration in Tp-CDs indicated that the amidation reaction of pPD was easier than that of mPD [29]. Moreover, Tm1-CDs also exhibited characteristic absorption peaks of amide bonds. An absorption peak at 828 cm−1 for out-of-plane deformations of C-H in the 1,4-disubstituted benzene ring, suggests that Tp-CDs retain a few aniline groups [30]. The COC stretching vibration was observed at 1118 and 1075 cm−1 in Tm1-, Tm2-, and Tp-CDs, but not in Tma-CDs [31]. The CN stretching vibration of aliphatic amines was observed at 1225 and 1254 cm−1 in Tm2- and Tma-CDs [29].

The wide-scan XPS spectra demonstrated that the prepared CDs all consisted of C, N, and O (Fig. S1). Compared to CDs synthesized by the hydrothermal method, Tma-CDs synthesized via the solvothermal method had the highest content of C and the lowest content of N and O. It further indicated that acetone enhanced the dehydration polymerization, leading to the formation of more aliphatic groups on the surface of Tma-CDs. Electronegative atoms were more likely to be retained during hydrothermal polymerization, facilitating the enrichment of O- and N- groups on the surface of hydrothermally synthesized CDs. The substitution of pPD for mPD in the synthesis of Tp-CDs resulted in the highest total N content, demonstrating effective modulation of N-doping.

In the high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 2 a), Tp-CDs exhibited the highest levels of CO and CN but a lower –COOH level, in agreement with FT-IR characterization, further confirming easier amidation reaction of pPD than that of mPD. In the N1s spectra (Fig. 2b), the absence of a graphitic N peak in Tma-CDs suggested that acetone-induced high pressure prevented graphitic N doping [32]. Additionally, hydrothermally synthesized Tm1-, Tm2-, and Tp-CDs revealed an increase in graphitic N content, which may lead to a red shift in fluorescent emission spectra [33]. In the O1s spectra (Fig. 2c), stronger intensity of COC was detected in Tm2- and Tp-CDs, in agreement with FT-IR characterization, further confirming that Tm2- and Tp-CDs possessed abundant ether bonds.

The molecular composition and bonding patterns of these CDs were furtherly demonstrated by probing the carbon and hydrogen atoms through 13C and 1H NMR (Fig. 2d and e) [34,35]. The 13C and 1H NMR spectrum of Tma-CDs displayed the notable peaks within 35–50 and 1–3 ppm, respectively, confirming the enrichment of aliphatic carbons and protons in Tma-CDs. The significantly remarkable signals within 100-140 ppm in 13C NMR and 6–8 ppm in 1H NMR spectra of Tp-CDs ascribed to aromatic sp2-hybridized carbons and aromatic protons, respectively, furtherly confirmed the largest sized aromatic sp2 conjugated domains of Tp-CDs. The peaks of CC/CN/CO within 55–75 ppm in 13C NMR spectra and of protons attached to –OH/–NH2/-O-/CO within 3–5 ppm in 1H NMR spectra demonstrated O-/N- functional groups enriched in all the CDs. Also, the 13C NMR spectra of Tm1- and Tp-CDs both displayed significant signals within 170–180 ppm assigned to carbonyl sp2-hybridized carbons, that was consistent with the results of FT-IR and XPS.

The n-octanol/water partition coefficients (log P) of Tm1-, Tm2-, Tma-, and Tp-CDs were −0.78, −2.17, 0.42, and −2.43, respectively, suggesting the hydrophilicity of Tm2- and Tp-CDs. Moreover, Tm1- and Tma-CDs displayed amphipathy and could dissolve in both n-octanol and water, the former favored a more water-soluble environment, while the latter preferred a lip-soluble environment. The O- and N- groups, such as hydroxyl group and amine group, endow each CDs with water-solubility, while the fat-solubility of Tm1- and Tma-CDs were due to abundant aliphatic hydrocarbon groups.

The varied ζ potential of these CDs were determined as shown in Fig. S2, which was due to protonation or deprotonation of surface functional groups at different pH values. As pH decreased, amine groups, pyridinic N and aniline –NH2 groups successively protonated [32,36,37]. At pH 7.4, the ζ potential of Tm2- and Tma-CDs was significantly higher than that of Tm1- and Tp-CDs, which may have been caused by protonation of abundant amine groups on the surface of Tm2- and Tma-CDs. When the pH was decreased to 5.0, protonated aniline –NH2 groups, unique to Tp-CDs as determined by FT-IR analysis, resulted in large accumulation of surface positive charge.

The schematic diagrams of these CDs rich in O-/N- groups were shown as Fig. 2f. The copolymer heterogeneity of the precursors results in more aliphatic and amide groups on Tm1-CDs, while more amine and ether bonds on Tm2-CDs. Tma-CDs exhibited an abundance of aliphatic and amine groups but no ether bonds on the surface, and no graphitic N in the carbon core. Tp-CDs with numerous amide and ether bonds, retained a significant amount of aromatic amine and exhibited the highest graphitic N doping. Also, the particle sizes and the conjugated sp2 domains of the four CDs increase in the order of Tm1-, Tm2-, Tma-, and Tp-CDs.

Optical properties and their luminescence mechanism were analyzed by the 3D fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy. In 3D fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3 a–d), Tm1-, Tm2-, Tma-, and Tp-CDs all exhibited the excitation-independent emission characteristic, emitting blue, green, and yellow fluorescence under a UV light of 365 nm. Their emission centers were located at λex/λem = 341/421, 445/514, 427/509, and 385/552 nm, with full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 62, 76, 53, and 94 nm, respectively. The fluorescence quantum yields (FLQY) were found to be 28.6 %, 23.3 %, 47.8 %, and 19.1 %, respectively. Tma-CDs showed the highest FLQY and narrowest FWHM indicating their narrow-bandwidth emissive characteristics. It may be due to the minimal O content (13.1 %), which reduces nonradiative electron-hole pair coupling and bandgap fluctuations, enhancing QY values and narrow linewidth emission [38,39].

In UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 3e), these CDs exhibited absorption peaks around 210 and 250 nm, corresponding to π–π* transitions of CC in aromatic sp2 domains [40,41]. Absorption peaks attributed to n-π* transitions from CN are assigned as the edge state from 280 to 310 nm, indicating the presence of edge carbon-connected pyrrole and pyridine structures [42]. The broad peaks at 340 and 360 nm are ascribed to the n-π* transitions of the CO/CN bonds, observed for Tm1- and Tp-CDs, which is in agreement with the presence of more amide groups (–CONH-) in Tm1- and Tp-CDs as analyzed by FT-IR spectra [43]. While the absorption tail of Tm2- and Tma-CDs around 450 nm may stem from the amine groups, which control the surface state in CDs [44]. It was in agreement with the result of FT-IR that Tm2- and Tma-CDs possessed more aliphatic amine groups. The distinct absorption behaviors of these CDs may be caused by different aromatic sp2 domains and surface groups.

Differences in the graphitized carbon core structures of these CDs may be the primary cause of the fluorescence redshift (Fig. 3f), which can be attributed to the reduction of the energy gap between the lowest unoccupied and highest occupied molecular orbitals (LUMO and HOMO) resulting from variations in core graphitization. The underlying mechanism can be explained by the synergistic effect of the quantum confinement effect and heteroatom doping effect in the carbon core structure [22,45]. The former suggests that an increase in the size of the graphitized conjugated sp2 domain enhances electron dispersion and mobility, while the latter indicates that graphitic N in the carbon core introduces intermediate energy states [33,46]. The combined effect of these factors leads to a decrease in the effective gap between LUMO and HOMO, resulting in varying degrees of redshift emission. Based on the theory, Tm1-, Tm2-, Tma-, and Tp-CDs show an increase in conjugated sp2 domains as gradually enhanced graphitization and diameters expand from 2.9 to 7.2 nm. Additionally, the relative graphitic N content in Tm1-, Tm2-, and Tp-CDs rises from 10.7 % to 19.3 %, leading to a redshift in fluorescence from blue (421 nm) to yellow (552 nm). Compared to that of Tm2-CDs (514 nm), the limited redshift of Tma-CDs emission center (509 nm) may be due to lack of graphitic N in Tma-CDs.

To exploit their potential application in cell imaging, Stokes shift and fluorescence stability were determined. The Stokes shifts of these CDs were 80, 69, 82, and 167 nm, respectively. The larger Stokes shift may help to diminish signal-to-noise ratio and self-quenching effects for cellular fluorescence imaging [26]. The fluorescence intensity of these CDs all maintained stable over a wide pH range of 3–11 (Fig. S3a). The fluorescence intensity of these CDs was reduced by 15 % at maximum after irradiation with a xenon lamp for 3 h (Fig. S3b). Also, except for Fe2+ and Fe3+, Ca2+, Na+, K+, Al3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Mg2+, and Ni2+ caused no obvious fluorescence intensity changes of these CDs (Fig. S3c). Furthermore, no significant differences in fluorescence intensity among aqueous, PBS and DMEM solution of CDs were observed (Fig. S3d). The results indicated that these CDs showed low signal background and fluorescence stability.

For fluorescence bio-imaging application, the viability of human cells (MCF-7 and HUVEC), mouse cells (NIH-3T3), bacteria (Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus), and fungus (Candida albicans) after CDs treatment was determined, showing low toxicity of these CDs to various cells (Fig. S4).

Confocal fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells treated with Tm-, Tm1-, Tm2-, Tma-, and Tp-CDs showed different fluorescent colors and intracellular distributions (Fig. 4 a). Tm-CDs emitted dual fluorescence signals at the blue and green channels, which localized in the cytoplasm and nucleus, respectively. Meanwhile, Tm1-, Tm2-, Tma-, and Tp-CDs showed blue fluorescence in the cytoplasm, green fluorescence in the nucleus, green fluorescence in the cytoplasm, and yellow fluorescence in the nucleus, respectively, demonstrating their excellent single-channel imaging capability.

Fluorescence imaging and quantitative analysis (Fig. 4b) demonstrated the subcellular co-localization of Tm1-CDs with ER-Tracker Red, Tm2- and Tp-CDs with Hoechst 33342, and Tma-CDs with Lyso-Tracker Red. Measurement of Pearson correlation coefficients (PCCs) showed the high spatial correlation (0.86–0.96) between CDs and co-localized commercial organelle dyes in various cells (Figs. S5 and 4c), whereas the low correlation (0.09–0.48) between CDs and other organelle dyes (Figs. S6 and 4d). These results suggested that the specific organelle targeting of Tm1-CDs to ER, Tm2- and Tp-CDs to the nucleus, and Tma-CDs to lysosomes.

The co-localization of blue fluorescence signal of Tm-CDs with ER-Tracker Red and green fluorescence signal of Tm-CDs with the nuclear dye DRAQ5 was observed in Fig. S7. The dual-color emission has been shown to be determined by the differences in carbon core structures of Tm-CDs mixtures, while the different surface properties determined their distinct organelle targeting capabilities. Based on the differences in surface characteristics, Tm1- and Tm2-CDs were thoroughly separated as single-emitting components.

Cellular uptake of these CDs was assessed in MCF-7 cells through endocytosis inhibition assays. Inhibitory effects of pharmaceuticals on endocytosis were detailed in Table S1 [47]. As shown in Fig. 5 , cellular uptake of these CDs was all significantly inhibited by 2-DG, indicating their energy-consumed uptake process. Tm1-CDs uptake was blocked by GEN, indicating caveolin-mediated endocytosis. Uptake of Tm2-, Tma-, and Tp-CDs was both inhibited by CPZ, suggesting slathrin-mediated endocytosis.

Upon entering cells via passive diffusion, dye molecules directly reach the target organelles, whereas CDs, after endocytosis, require intracellular transport to arrive at their targets. Intracellular transportpathways of these CDs were analyzed by time-lapse imaging of MCF-7 cells co-stained with specific organelle dyes. Enrichment of Tm1-, Tm2-, Tma-, and Tp-CDs to targeted organelles required 40, 20, 20, and 120 min, respectively. Tm1- and Tma-CDs always co-localized with ER and lysosome dye (Fig. 6 a and 6b). While Tm2-CDs showed first cytoplasmic localization and then nuclear localization (Fig. 6c). More obviously, Tp-CDs first co-localized with lysosome dye and then with nucleus dye (Fig. 6d). This suggested that upon cellular entry, Tm1- and Tma-CDs were directly transported to their target organelles via vesicular transport, Tm2- and Tp-CDs were targeted to nucleus following by endosomal/lysosomal escape [48,49]. During endosomes/lysosomes traffic, luminal acidity increased from pH 6.5 for early endosomes, pH 5.5 for late endosomes to pH 4.5 for lysosomes [50,51]. Due to protonation of functional groups at specific pH, Tm2- and Tp-CDs exhibited different endosomal/lysosomal escape capability according to the “proton sponge” hypothesis. Thus, Tm2-CDs, enriched with amine groups and pyridine N, quickly escaped from endosomes through protonation at the higher pH of 5.5 to 6.5. Due to the protonation of aromatic amine groups at the lower pH, Tp-CDs could only escape from lysosomes (pH 4.5 to 5.0).

Single organelle targeted fluorescent probes are crucial for tracing the dynamic location and morphological characteristics of organelles. The conventional targeting strategy is designed based on the structure and functional characteristics of organelles, such as organelle-targeting small molecules and organelle-penetrating peptides [52,53]. Some nanomaterials targeting specific organelles rely on their intrinsic physiochemical properties. However, due to the uncertainty of intracellular distribution and mechanism, the bio-imaging application of these nanomaterials is still lagging behind. Multiple factors contribute to this uncertainty, including physicochemical properties of nanomaterials, biochemical properties of organelles, and nano-bio interactions [54].

As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the targeting mechanisms of the prepared CDs to specific organelles were analyzed. CDs must undergo cellular uptake and intracellular trafficking before precise organelle targeting. First, multiple endocytosis-based uptake pathways were selected by CDs according to their surface charges. Positively charged Tm2-, Tma-, and Tp-CDs enhance electrostatic adsorption to negatively charged cell membrane receptors, facilitating vesicle formation and clathrin-mediated endocytosis [55,56]. While, neutrally charged Tm1-CDs participate in caveolae-mediated endocytosis, potentially due to enhancing cholesterol clustering and membrane curvature [57]. Second, after cellular internalization, CDs underwent different trafficking routes determined by endocytosis mode and surface chemistry. Generally, intracellular destinations of caveolin-coated vesicles include the Golgi apparatus and ER [54]. Here, caveolin-coated vesicles transported Tm1-CDs through the cytoplasm to ER. Clathrin-coated vesicles transported Tm2-, Tma-, and Tp-CDs to endosomes. During subsequent endo-lysosomal trafficking, hydrophilic Tm2- and Tp-CDs escaped from endosomes and lysosomes, respectively. While cationic amphiphilic Tma-CDs were sequestrated in lysosomes. Last, after endo-lysosomal escape, Tm2- and Tp-CDs targeted to the nucleus. Positive electricity, particle size smaller than nuclear pore complex and abundant ether bonds may contribute to facilitating Tm2- and Tp-CDs nuclear passive diffusion and enhancing their stabilization and enrichment within nuclear biomolecules [58–60].

Low pH-induced to ER membrane permeability was monitored by Tm1-CDs. At pH 4.5, blue fluorescence of Tm1-CDs in the ER decreased with time and completely disappeared within 1 h, while red fluorescence of PI (cell-membrane impermeable dye for nucleus) in the nucleus gradually increased (Fig. 7 a). In contrast, blue fluorescence intensity of Tm1-CDs stained cells maintained stable at pH 7.4 (Fig. S8a). This suggested that diminished fluorescence of Tm1-CDs could indicate the change of ER membrane permeability just as increased fluorescence of PI for indication of cell membrane permeability. Tm2-CDs, with high specificity and affinity for nucleic acid, could illustrate the progressive stages of mitosis in MCF-7 cell (Fig. 7b and S8b). Lysosomal movement could be monitored by Tma-CDs staining (Fig. 7c). Also, during staining for 48 h, Tma-CDs always localized to lysosomes and emitted brightly green fluorescence (Fig. S8c). This indicated that Tma-CDs was suitable for real-time and long-term tracking of lysosomes.

Fluorescence imaging of bacterial, fungal and plant live cells using CDs were determined. These CDs all displayed the whole bacterial cell staining, yet could not discriminate bacteria (Fig. 8 a and Fig. S9). Co-staining P. aeruginosa with Tm1-, Tm2-, and Tp-CDs showed blue, green, and yellow fluorescence (Fig. 8b), respectively, indicating their potential application in three-channel fluorescence visualization of live bacteria. Fluorescence images of C. albicans and S. cerevisiae showed that Tm1-, Tm2-, and Tma-CDs localized in the whole cell, nucleus and vacuoles (functionally similar to mammalian lysosomes), respectively, while, Tp-CDs could not pass through the fungal cell wall (Fig. 8c and Fig. S10). The localization of Tm2- and Tma-CDs to nucleus and vacuoles in fungal cells was the same as that to nucleus and lysosomes in human cells. CDs have been reported to specifically lable the cell walls of bacteria and fungi in previous studies [61–63]. And the fungal intracellular organelle-targeted imaging of CDs is the first reported here. In plant cell imaging, the onion epidermal cells staining showed that Tm1- and Tp-CDs could target to cell wall, while Tm2- and Tma-CDs accumulated into the nucleus, dispersed throughout the cells, respectively (Fig. 8d and Fig. S11). To sum up, the four CDs could be widely applied for single-channel fluorescence imaging of live bacteria, fungi and plant cells. Among them, Tm1-, Tm2-, and Tp-CDs could be used for simultaneous multicolor imaging of live bacteria; Tm2-CDs could be used to label nucleus in fungal and plant live cells; Tma-CDs showed specifically vacuoles-targeted imaging of live fungi; Tm1- and Tp-CDs showed plant cell wall-targeted imaging.

In summary, four CDs (Tm1-, Tm2-, Tma-, and Tp-CDs) with different core graphitization and surface groups were obtained using TA and mPD/pPD through hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis. Improved productivity and QY will bring about hope for large-scale production of CDs. These CDs emit excitation-independent blue, green, and yellow fluorescence, respectively. Increased conjugated sp2 domain and graphitic N content of carbon core cause the red shift of fluorescence. Rationally designed surface physicochemical properties of CDs enable diverse uptake pathways and intracellular transport, allowing for precise targeting of different organelles. Specifically, in culture medium, Tm1-CDs with –CH3, C-O-C, and –CONH- groups, characterized by the amphiphilic and neutral charged, are localized in the ER. Tma-CDs with –CH3 and –NH2 groups, characterized by the amphiphilic and positively charged, are localized in the lysosomes. Tm2- with C-O-C and –NH2 groups, and Tp-CDs with C-O-C, –CONH-, and aniline –NH2 groups, both characterized by high hydrophilicity and positive charged, can escape from endosomes/lysosomes and ultimately localize in the nucleus. These CDs have the potential application in organelle-targeted imaging in living mammalian, fungal and plant cells. This study elucidates the structure–property relationships and the luminescence and organelle-targeting mechanisms of the CDs. It provides novel insights for rational design of multi-color emissive fluorescent CDs targeting to specific organelle of live cells.

Ming-Yuan Sun: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Bao-Hua Tian: Validation, Methodology. Xin-Xin Li: Validation, Methodology. Yu Li: Visualization, Methodology. Yu Lei: Visualization, Methodology. Xi-Lin Guo: Investigation. Qiang Miao: Investigation. Hao Li: Investigation. Hai-Xia Liang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from National Natural Scientific Foundation of China (No. 31501124), Key Research and Development Program (International Cooperation) in Shanxi Province of China (No. 201803D421087), Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shanxi Province of China (No. 202203021212197 and 202203021221046).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.153729.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
